target.title: fix(codex): stream non-final-answer assistant deltas as partials
target.body:
Fixes #95422

Codex partial-reply streaming was gated on `final_answer` phase, newer app-servers omit phase mid-stream, TUI/WebChat need `stream:"assistant"` agent events (not just `onPartialReply`), and switched assistant items need `replace: true` for append-oriented channel consumers.

## What Problem This Solves

Agents on the **OpenAI Codex** app-server runtime show no incremental streaming in TUI/WebChat — full reply in one chunk at turn end while Codex CLI streams normally. See #95422.

Three gaps in `CodexAppServerEventProjector.handleAssistantDelta`:

1. **Phase gate** — partials only when `phase === "final_answer"`; Codex `>= 0.139` streams unphased deltas (`f321307`).
2. **TUI path** — TUI/WebChat consume agent events → `emitChatDelta`, not `onPartialReply`; Codex only emitted one terminal assistant event (`f2647a1`).
3. **Item replacement** — multiple non-commentary items per turn need `replace: true` on item switch for Teams etc. (`874166e`).

## Why This Change Was Made

- Stream all non-commentary deltas via `onPartialReply` + assistant agent events.
- Mark `replace: true` when `itemId` switches so superseded coordination text is not concatenated.
- Turn completion still selects final reply; commentary stays progress-only.

## User Impact

- Incremental TUI/WebChat streaming for Codex-runtime agents.
- Safe partial delivery for append-oriented channel consumers on multi-item turns.

## Evidence

**Live TUI proof:** before/after recordings in [comment #4760200267](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95404#issuecomment-4760200267).

| Stage | emitChatDelta sends | TUI |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Before PR | 1 full chunk | one chunk |
| After commit 1 | 1 full chunk | one chunk |
| After commits 2+3 | ~100 incremental | streaming ✓ |

**Gateway probes + setup:** [comment #4760189655](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/pull/95404#issuecomment-4760189655).

**Tests:** `pnpm vitest run extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts` → **97/97**, including:
- `streams assistant deltas when the app-server omits the item phase`
- `marks partial replacement when an unphased intermediate item is superseded by a final item`

**Lint:** `npx oxlint extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.{ts,test.ts}` → clean.

target.title: fix(ui): change silent cron quick-create sessionTarget to isolated
target.body:
## Summary

The "Silent" delivery preset in the Cron quick-create dialog (`cron-quick-create.ts`) maps to `sessionTarget: "main"`, but the server-side validation in `assertSupportedJobSpec()` rejects `sessionTarget="main"` combined with `payloadKind="agentTurn"` (only `"systemEvent"` payloads are allowed in main sessions). Since the wizard always uses `agentTurn` payloads, the "Silent" option silently fails — `addCronJob` throws on the server, the error is swallowed by `withCronBusy`, and the dialog remains open with no visible feedback to the user.

The fix changes `sessionTarget` from `"main"` to `"isolated"` for the "Silent" preset. The `deliveryMode: "none"` already ensures no result is announced, preserving the silent semantics. The "Notify me" and "Independent session" presets are unaffected because they already use `sessionTarget: "isolated"`.

Fixes #95073

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Silent cron quick-create preset now successfully creates jobs instead of failing silently due to server-side sessionTarget validation

**Real environment tested**: Ubuntu 24.04, Node.js v24, pnpm 11.2.2, OpenClaw 2026.6.1 (upstream main aa479ac7d8)

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
pnpm test ui/src/ui/views/cron-quick-create.node.test.ts
pnpm test ui/src/ui/views/
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```
$ pnpm test ui/src/ui/views/cron-quick-create.node.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)   ← new test for silent preset passes

$ pnpm test ui/src/ui/views/
 Test Files  29 passed (29)
      Tests  297 passed (297)   ← all view tests pass, no regression
```

**Observed result after the fix**: The "Silent" preset now maps to `sessionTarget: "isolated"` which the server accepts with `payloadKind: "agentTurn"`. All 297 view tests pass with no regression.

**What was not tested**: Full Gateway integration test (the fix is a client-side mapping change; server validation already covers the isolated + agentTurn combination for all existing presets).

## Tests and validation

### Unit tests

```
$ pnpm test ui/src/ui/views/cron-quick-create.node.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
   Start at  06:13:24
   Duration  418ms

$ pnpm test ui/src/ui/views/
 Test Files  29 passed (29)
      Tests  297 passed (297)
   Start at  06:13:32
   Duration  17.89s
```

### Root cause

1. `draftToCronFormPatch()` in `cron-quick-create.ts` maps "silent" → `sessionTarget: "main"`, `deliveryMode: "none"`
2. The mapped form state is merged with `DEFAULT_CRON_FORM` which has `payloadKind: "agentTurn"`
3. `addCronJob()` sends the job to the server via `client.request("cron.add", job)`
4. Server `assertSupportedJobSpec()` in `jobs.ts:286` throws: `'main cron jobs require payload.kind="systemEvent"'`
5. The error is caught by `withCronBusy()` → `state.cronError` is set, `saved = false`
6. The dialog stays open because `saved` is false — no visible feedback

The fix changes line 192 from `"main"` to `"isolated"`, matching what the server accepts.

## Risk checklist

- [x] This change is backwards compatible — older cron jobs are unaffected; only the quick-create mapping for the "Silent" option changes
- [x] This change has been tested with existing configurations — all 297 view tests pass
- [ ] I have updated relevant documentation — N/A, the fix is a one-line mapping correction
- [x] Breaking changes (if any) are documented in Summary — no breaking changes

## Current review state

- [x] Self-review completed
- [ ] At least one maintainer review
- [ ] All CI checks passed

target.title: fix(agents): auto-correct hallucinated document extensions in tool path params
target.body:
## Summary

- Models sometimes hallucinate document extensions by conflating Office file types with the "codex" token (`.docodex`, `.pptcodex`, `.xlscodex` instead of `.docx`, `.pptx`, `.xlsx`).
- These corrupted paths pass through the existing `wrapToolParamValidation` pipeline unchanged, causing file-tool operations (`read`, `write`, `edit`) to fail against non-existent filenames.
- This PR adds `normalizeHallucinatedDocumentExtension()` to detect and auto-correct these patterns, integrated into the shared validation pipeline alongside the existing XML suffix stripping.
- Covers all three file tools: write and edit via `wrapToolParamValidation`, read via `createOpenClawReadTool` (which has its own param normalization).
- Semantic choice: **silent auto-correct** — the hallucinated suffix is always a model error and never intentional; silently correcting avoids unnecessary retry overhead and lets the tool succeed on the real filename.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix / [ ] Feature / [ ] Refactor / [ ] Docs / [ ] Security / [ ] Chore

## Scope

- [ ] Gateway / [ ] Skills / [ ] Auth / [ ] Memory / [ ] Integrations / [x] API / [ ] UI/UX / [ ] CI

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** Models hallucinate `.docodex`/`.pptcodex`/`.xlscodex` extensions in tool call path parameters; these pass through validation unchanged and cause file operations to fail against non-existent filenames.

**Environment tested:** Node v24.13.1 on Linux, openclaw source at commit 70d8c7e4

**Steps run after the patch:**

```bash
node --import tsx --no-warnings -e "
import { normalizeHallucinatedDocumentExtension, wrapToolParamValidation, REQUIRED_PARAM_GROUPS } from './src/agents/agent-tools.params.js';
const execute = async (_id, args) => args;
const writeTool = wrapToolParamValidation({ name: 'write', label: 'write', description: 'write', parameters: {}, execute }, REQUIRED_PARAM_GROUPS.write);
const editTool = wrapToolParamValidation({ name: 'edit', label: 'edit', description: 'edit', parameters: {}, execute }, REQUIRED_PARAM_GROUPS.edit);
const readTool = wrapToolParamValidation({ name: 'read', label: 'read', description: 'read', parameters: {}, execute }, REQUIRED_PARAM_GROUPS.read);
const [wr, er, rr] = await Promise.all([
  writeTool.execute('id', { path: 'report.docodex', content: 'data' }),
  editTool.execute('id', { path: 'slides.pptcodex', edits: [{ oldText: 'a', newText: 'b' }] }),
  readTool.execute('id', { path: 'budget.xlscodex' }),
]);
console.log(JSON.stringify({ write: wr.path, edit: er.path, read: rr.path }, null, 2));
"
```

**Evidence after fix:**

Before (upstream/main — bug present, write path only; read/edit same behavior):

```text
write .docodex → report.docodex  (hallucinated extension passes through unchanged)
```

After (fix branch — all three tool types corrected):

```text
{
  "write": "report.docx",
  "edit": "slides.pptx",
  "read": "budget.xlsx"
}
```

**Observed result after the fix:** All three file tools (read, write, edit) auto-correct `.docodex`/`.pptcodex`/`.xlscodex` to `.docx`/`.pptx`/`.xlsx` before execution. Normal extensions pass through unchanged.

**What was not tested:** Live gateway session with a real model producing hallucinated extensions; shell-command paths (the fix only addresses structured file-tool path parameters, not exec tool commands).

## Root Cause

- Root cause: Models conflate Office extensions with the "codex" token during tool-call generation, producing non-existent file extensions.
- Missing detection/guardrail: The `wrapToolParamValidation` pipeline and `createOpenClawReadTool` stripped XML suffix corruption but had no guard against hallucinated document extensions.

## Regression Test Plan

- Target: `src/agents/agent-tools.params.test.ts` — `normalizeHallucinatedDocumentExtension` unit tests covering all three patterns, case-insensitivity, edge cases.
- Target: `src/agents/agent-tools.params.test.ts` — `wrapToolParamValidation with hallucinated document extension normalization` integration tests covering write, edit, read, XML-suffix and extension combo, and no-change paths.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users will see `.docodex`/`.pptcodex`/`.xlscodex` paths in tool calls silently corrected to `.docx`/`.pptx`/`.xlsx`, allowing file operations to succeed against the intended filenames.

## Security Impact

- New permissions? No
- Secrets handling changed? No
- New network calls? No

Closes #93326

target.title: fix(codex): suppress raw commentary echo when phase field is absent
target.body:
## Summary

In WebChat direct conversations with Codex-backed providers (e.g. `openai-codex/gpt-5.4`), internal commentary text such as `"commentary to=functions.browser ..."` and browser tool-call debug fragments leaked into the user-visible chat bubble.

The root cause was in the Codex app-server event projector's `handleRawResponseItemCompleted`. When a typed commentary item completed (incrementing `pendingRawCommentaryEchoes`), the paired raw response echo from the Codex app-server could arrive **without** a `phase` field. The suppression guard only matched when `phase === "commentary"`, so the un-phased raw item fell through — its text was stored without a phase annotation in `assistantPhaseByItem`, and `resolveFinalAssistantTextItem` included it in the visible reply because `undefined !== "commentary"`.

The fix adds a conservative fallback: when `pendingRawCommentaryEchoes > 0` but the raw item has no `phase`, the raw item is also suppressed. This matches the existing deduplication contract — one typed commentary echo consumes one raw counterpart.

Fixes #89668

## What Problem This Solves

Codex model turns that use the browser tool generate commentary text describing the tool call. This text is internal scaffolding and should never be visible to end users. When it leaks, it pollutes assistant replies with confusing/embarrassing garbage and can make it look like OpenClaw inserted ads into the answer.

## Evidence

The fix adds 2 lines of logic + tests. The existing test suite for Codex comment echo deduplication covers the happy path (raw item WITH phase). The new test verifies the fix for the edge case (raw item WITHOUT phase).

Before the fix:
- Typed commentary completion echoes as preamble ✅
- Raw echo WITH `phase: "commentary"` is suppressed ✅  
- Raw echo WITHOUT `phase` field leaks ❌ → **BUG**

After the fix:
- All three cases correctly suppress the echo

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Raw commentary echoes shipped without a `phase` field by the Codex app-server leaked into visible assistant text, appearing in WebChat as `"commentary to=functions.browser"` and tool-call scaffolding fragments.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node.js v24.13.1, Linux x86_64
- **Test framework**: Vitest v4.1.8
- **Branch**: `fix/issue-89668`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
cd /home/0668001085/workspace/openclaw
git checkout fix/issue-89668
npx vitest run extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

The key code change in `extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.ts`:

```typescript
// FIX #89668: The Codex app-server may ship raw response echoes for
// commentary items without a phase field.  When a typed commentary
// completion was already echoed, conservatively suppress the paired
// raw item even when its phase is absent, so the commentary text
// cannot leak into visible assistant output.
if (!phase && this.pendingRawCommentaryEchoes > 0) {
  this.pendingRawCommentaryEchoes -= 1;
  return;
}
```

Test scenario verified:
```typescript
// Typed commentary completion → pendingRawCommentaryEchoes = 1
// Raw response echo without phase → suppressed (FIX)
// Later raw-only note → delivered normally
expect(preambles.map(event => event.data.progressText)).toEqual([
  "Checking the workspace",  // typed echo
  "Still working",           // distinct later note
]);
```

**Observed result after the fix**: Raw commentary echoes missing a `phase` field are consumed by the existing echo counter instead of falling through to `assistantTextByItem`. Commentary text stays hidden. Distinct raw-only notes after the echo are still delivered correctly.

**What was not tested**: Live WebChat session with Codex + browser tool against a real endpoint. The fix is at the event-projection layer; it corrects a deterministic counter-mismatch that is reproducible in unit tests.

## Risk checklist

**Did user-visible behavior change?** `No` — only removes leaked commentary text that should never have been visible. All correctly-phased commentary items continue to be suppressed as before.

**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** `No`.

**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** `No`.

**What is the highest-risk area?** The conservative suppression could theoretically eat a raw response item that was NOT a commentary echo if the counter was incremented by a different mechanism.

**How is that risk mitigated?** `pendingRawCommentaryEchoes` is only incremented by the typed `agentMessage` commentary completion handler (line 659), and only for items with `phase: "commentary"`. Each increment has an exact 1:1 correspondence with a raw echo. The suppression only triggers when the counter is positive, matching the existing contract.

## Current review state

**What is the next action?** Maintainer review requested.

target.title: feat(docs): add docs_map.md for LLM navigation (fixes #69442)
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

OpenClaw has 670+ doc pages across automation/, channels/, cli/, concepts/, gateway/, install/, nodes/, plugins/, providers/, tools/, web/, and more. LLM agents currently have to discover this tree through trial and error. A single `docs_map.md` fixes that by providing a structured outline of every page's headings.

This follows the pattern established by [code.claude.com/docs/en/claude_code_docs_map.md](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/claude_code_docs_map.md) — a docs map that lets an AI read one file and know exactly what concepts are inside each page.

## Changes

- Add `scripts/generate-docs-map.mjs` — walks `docs/`, extracts front-matter title and H2/H3/H4 headings, outputs structured `docs/docs_map.md`
- Add `pnpm docs:map` script to `package.json`
- Generate `docs/docs_map.md` covering 671 pages with 4672 headings

## How to Regenerate

```bash
pnpm docs:map
```

- [x] AI-assisted (Hermes Agent)

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** Adds a docs_map.md navigation file for LLM agents to discover doc pages by heading outline
- **Environment tested:** macOS, Node.js, OpenClaw repo at commit a824df2e
- **Steps run after the patch:** Ran `pnpm docs:map` to generate docs/docs_map.md; ran `pnpm build` to verify no build regression
- **Evidence after fix:**
```
$ node -e "const fs=require('fs'); const c=fs.readFileSync('docs/docs_map.md','utf8'); console.log('pages:', (c.match(/### /g)||[]).length); console.log('headings:', (c.match(/^- /gm)||[]).length); console.log('has_header:', c.startsWith('# Docs Map'));"
pages: 671
headings: 4672
has_header: true
$ grep -n 'generate-docs-map' package.json
1574:    "docs:map": "node scripts/generate-docs-map.mjs",
```
- **Observed result after fix:** docs/docs_map.md is generated with 671 pages and 4672 headings; `pnpm docs:map` script is registered in package.json; `pnpm build` passes
- **What was not tested:** CI workflow integration (auto-regeneration on docs/** push) — left as a follow-up since it requires GitHub Actions changes

target.title: fix(diagnostic): lower stall abort threshold and extend model-call recovery to CLI sessions
target.body:
## Summary

The diagnostic heartbeat detects stalled sessions (model calls stuck >120s) but the abort threshold was 360s (6 minutes) — far too long to recover from a hung provider. Additionally, `isStalledModelCallRecoveryEligible` required `hasActiveEmbeddedRun === true`, which excluded CLI harness sessions (e.g. Codex-backed providers) from model-call recovery entirely.

This fix addresses the root cause at the activity API layer by introducing `hasActiveModelCall` to the `DiagnosticSessionActivitySnapshot`. Unlike `hasActiveEmbeddedRun` (which only tracks sessions that called `markDiagnosticEmbeddedRunStarted` — embedded-run and auto-reply code paths), `hasActiveModelCall` is driven by `activeModelCalls.size`, which `recordModelStarted` populates for **all** session types when the `model.call.started` diagnostic event fires. This means CLI harness, embedded-run, and reply sessions all correctly report model-call activity.

Changes:
1. `diagnostic-run-activity.ts`: add `hasActiveModelCall` to the snapshot type and builder, keyed on `activeModelCalls.size > 0`
2. `diagnostic-session-attention.ts`: classify `model_call` activity with the new field so non-embedded sessions are recognized
3. `diagnostic.ts` (`isStalledModelCallRecoveryEligible`): use `hasActiveModelCall` uniformly — no 2× safety hack, no session-type branching
4. `MIN_STALLED_EMBEDDED_RUN_ABORT_MS`: 300s → 180s; multiplier: 3× → 2×

Fixes #94650

## What Problem This Solves

Gateway diagnostic heartbeat detects stalled model calls (no progress for >120s) but the abort threshold defaulted to 360s. The old logic also required `hasActiveEmbeddedRun === true` in the activity snapshot, which only embedded-run and auto-reply sessions set — CLI harness sessions (Codex, direct CLI usage) were permanently excluded from model-call stall recovery.

This PR fixes the problem at two levels:
- **API layer**: `hasActiveModelCall` now accurately reports whether any session type has an active model call, regardless of whether it also has an active embedded run
- **Thresholds**: lowered abort floor from 300s→180s and multiplier from 3×→2× so hung calls abort at ~240s instead of ~360s

## Evidence

After merging two commits (original fix + API-layer refactor), the diagnostic test suite passes with coverage for both non-embedded model-call classification and active-abort recovery:

```
$ npx vitest run src/logging/diagnostic.test.ts src/logging/diagnostic-session-attention.test.ts

 ✓ diagnostic-session-attention.test.ts (11 tests)
 ✓ diagnostic.test.ts (70 tests)
```

Key behavioral proof:

| Scenario | Before | After |
|----------|--------|-------|
| CLI model call, no progress > abort threshold | `recovery=none` ❌ | `abortStalledModelCall` ✅ |
| Embedded model call, progress within threshold | no abort ✅ | no abort ✅ (unchanged) |
| Malformed `stuckSessionAbortMs` config | falls back to 300s | falls back to 180s |
| `resolveStuckSessionAbortMs(undefined, 30_000)` | `300_000` | `180_000` |
| Orphaned model_call + idle + queue | stale-state recover | stale-state recover (unchanged) |

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Stalled model calls in non-embedded sessions (CLI harness, Codex-backed providers) were never recovered because `hasActiveEmbeddedRun` was false. Embedded-run sessions had recovery gated at 360s — far too long.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node.js v24.13.1, Linux x86_64
- **Test framework**: Vitest v4.1.8
- **Branch**: `fix/issue-94650-gateway-watchdog-stall-recovery`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
cd /home/0668001085/workspace/openclaw
git checkout fix/issue-94650-gateway-watchdog-stall-recovery
npx vitest run src/logging/diagnostic.test.ts src/logging/diagnostic-session-attention.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  81 passed (81)
   Duration  15.18s
```

**Observed result after the fix**: Non-embedded model calls are now correctly classified as `stalled_agent_run` and recovered via active abort when the session exceeds the abort threshold. The single `hasActiveModelCall` field cleanly replaces the embedded-vs-CLI branching without a 2× safety hack.

**What was not tested**: Live hung model call with a real provider. The activity lifecycle is exercised through vitest fake timers and diagnostic event injection, which covers the same state machine paths.

## Risk checklist

**Did user-visible behavior change?** `No` — only changes internal watchdog timing and extends recovery to previously-uncovered session types.

**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** `No` — users can still override via `diagnostics.stuckSessionAbortMs`.

**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** `No`.

**What is the highest-risk area?** Lowering the abort threshold could cause premature aborts.

**How is that risk mitigated?** The 240s default is generous for modern API calls (most complete in 30-60s). Operators with slow providers can set `diagnostics.stuckSessionAbortMs` higher.

## Current review state

**What is the next action?** Maintainer review requested. The API-layer refactor addresses the root cause rather than symptom-patching.

target.title: fix #95351: [Feature]: Generic JSONL line-parsing hook for CliBackendPlugin (native tool-card support beyond claude-stream-json)
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `CliBackendPlugin` backends that use `output: "jsonl"` had no public, backend-owned parser hook for provider-specific JSONL streams, so third-party CLIs with native tool execution could not feed OpenClaw's tool-card event path unless they impersonated a built-in dialect.
- Solution: Add an opt-in `parseJsonlEvent(line, ctx)` plugin hook that normalizes backend-native JSONL lines into existing assistant, thinking, result, session id, and display-only native tool events.
- What changed: The hook is exported through the plugin SDK, preserved through CLI backend resolution, passed into the CLI runner, and handled by `createCliJsonlStreamingParser` before built-in Claude/Gemini JSONL parsing. Plugin hook tool events now use display-only tool-card callbacks so they do not mutate messaging delivery evidence. Focused tests cover hook capability, backend resolution, a Cursor-like parser stream, and the display-only runner path.
- What did NOT change: This does not change the `agents.defaults.cliBackends.*.jsonlDialect` config schema, does not add a new built-in dialect value, does not execute plugin-reported tools through OpenClaw's tool catalog, does not let plugin display events count as message delivery evidence, and does not change Claude/Gemini stream-json semantics.
- Why it matters / User impact: Plugin authors can build backend integrations for CLIs such as Cursor Agent that already run their own tools and still present collapsible native tool cards in the TUI/Control UI instead of degrading those events into plain text.
- Fixes #95351

## What Problem This Solves

Third-party `registerCliBackend` integrations can already launch JSONL-emitting CLIs, but OpenClaw's native tool-card path was tied to built-in Claude/Gemini stream dialects. A plugin with a different wire format had no canonical public contract for mapping already-executed backend tool calls to OpenClaw display events. This PR creates that contract while keeping provider-specific parsing owned by the backend plugin.

## Evidence

Local verification covered the parser behavior, backend resolution path, public SDK export surface, API baseline update, display-only runner isolation, lint/format cleanliness, and strict build smoke. The focused parser regression drives a Cursor-like JSONL sequence through `createCliJsonlStreamingParser` and observes assistant deltas, thinking commentary, native tool start/result deltas, final text, session id, and usage. The runner regression drives a plugin JSONL `message` tool card through `executePreparedCliRun` and verifies it renders tool events without setting messaging delivery evidence.

## Real behavior proof
- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A plugin-owned CLI backend with `output: "jsonl"` can now translate provider-specific JSONL lines into OpenClaw display events, including native tool start/result cards, without selecting a built-in Claude or Gemini dialect.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on Windows/Git Bash with Node v22.17.0. The repository reports an engine warning for `>=22.19.0`, but the focused tests and build/lint/format checks below completed successfully in this environment.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran `pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/cli-output.test.ts src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts src/agents/cli-runner/execute.supervisor-capture.test.ts`, `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen`, `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`, `pnpm build:plugin-sdk:strict-smoke`, `pnpm exec oxlint` on the changed TypeScript files, `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` on the changed source/doc files plus the SDK API baseline hash, `git diff --check`, and `pnpm build:strict-smoke`.
- **Evidence after fix:** Vitest reported `Test Files 5 passed (5)` and `Tests 156 passed (156)` for the focused suite. `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check` reported `OK docs\\.generated\\plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` after regenerating the baseline. The plugin SDK strict smoke reported `CLI bootstrap import guard passed.` and `OK: All 4 required plugin-sdk exports verified.` Format check reported `All matched files use the correct format.` The strict build smoke also completed with the same CLI bootstrap and plugin SDK export checks.
- **Observed result after fix:** The parser test emits the expected accumulated assistant text, thinking commentary, `read_file` tool start delta, matching tool result delta, final result text, session id, and usage. The backend resolution test confirms the exact `parseJsonlEvent` function registered by a runtime backend survives `requireCliBackendConfig`. The runner test confirms a plugin-parsed `message` tool card still emits display tool events but leaves `didSendViaMessagingTool` and messaging delivery evidence unset.
- **What was not tested:** I did not run a live external Cursor Agent CLI process or a full TUI/manual Control UI session. `pnpm check:changed` could not run because the local `crabbox` binary failed its basic sanity check before repo checks, and `pnpm check:test-types` stopped on an unrelated existing `packages/sdk/src/package.e2e.test.ts(127,5)` `string | URL` type error.

## Review findings addressed

- **RF-001 / RF-002 / RF-005 — Real plugin/UI proof request:** Not fully available in this local environment. The PR body now explicitly states that I did not run a live external Cursor Agent CLI process or full TUI/Control UI session. Completed local proof covers the source-level behavior with focused parser and runner regressions, including native tool-card event emission from plugin-parsed JSONL and display-only isolation. A maintainer/Mantis visual run is still the appropriate follow-up for UI proof.
- **RF-003 / RF-006 — Public plugin SDK API review / maintainer decision:** Maintainer API review is still required because this intentionally adds a public `CliBackendPlugin` hook and normalized event union. The contract is now documented as opt-in, backend-owned, display-only for tool events, and backed by an updated SDK API baseline plus focused tests.
- **RF-004 / RF-007 — Keep display-only tool events out of delivery tracking:** Fixed by splitting runner tool-card emission from messaging delivery evidence. Plugin hook tool events now route through display-only callbacks that emit native tool cards and mark CLI activity, but do not add `pendingMessagingCalls`, do not call `commitMessagingToolResult`, and do not set `didSendViaMessagingTool`. Added a regression in `src/agents/cli-runner/execute.supervisor-capture.test.ts` using a plugin-parsed `message` tool event to prove display cards render without delivery evidence.
- **RF-008 — Regenerate the Plugin SDK API baseline:** Fixed by running `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:gen` and committing the updated `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256`. Verified with `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`.

## Regression Test Plan
- **Target test file:** `src/agents/cli-output.test.ts`, `src/agents/cli-backends.test.ts`, and `src/agents/cli-runner/execute.supervisor-capture.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** The parser regression feeds a custom plugin hook a Cursor-like JSONL stream containing session initialization, thinking text, assistant text deltas, tool call started/completed records, and a final result. The assertions verify the normalized assistant/commentary/tool/result/session/usage outputs. The backend regression verifies a runtime backend's parser hook is preserved on the resolved backend config. The runner regression verifies plugin display tool events stay out of message-delivery evidence while still emitting native tool-card events.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** These tests exercise the source parser, resolver contract, and runner callback split directly, without requiring a live third-party CLI binary. They lock the new plugin hook behavior at the boundary where OpenClaw converts CLI stdout into existing native display events and where display-only events must not affect delivery/retry evidence.

## Merge risk
- **Risk labels considered:** compatibility, session-state, message-delivery, security-boundary, auth-provider, automation.
- **Risk explanation:** The real merge risk is compatibility/public contract risk: this adds a new plugin SDK hook and normalized event union. It is not an auth-provider or security-boundary behavior change, and plugin-reported tool events remain display-only; they do not grant tool execution authority, register tools, read credentials, change sandbox/permission enforcement, or count as message delivery/source-reply evidence. Session-state impact is limited to preserving a plugin-reported `sessionId` in the same parser state used by existing JSONL dialects. Message-delivery impact is now isolated to native tool-card display callbacks, with a regression proving plugin-reported `message` events do not set delivery evidence.
- **Why acceptable:** The hook is opt-in, backend-owned, and scoped to `output: "jsonl"` streams. Existing Claude/Gemini parsing remains separated behind explicit dialect checks, and unrecognized hook lines return `undefined`/`null` so existing parsing can continue. The public contract surface is small, documented, backed by an updated plugin SDK API baseline, and covered by focused regression tests plus plugin SDK export smoke.

## Root Cause
- **Root cause:** The source-of-truth parser contract for generic CLI JSONL output lived only inside OpenClaw's built-in stream dialect handling. `CliBackendPlugin` had public runtime hooks for execution setup and argv rewriting, but no equivalent parser hook was preserved through `ResolvedCliBackend` and consumed by the CLI runner. Because `jsonlDialect` is a config/schema-owned built-in enum, third-party plugin functions could not be represented there, leaving custom JSONL tool events outside the native tool-card event path.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** The patch adds the missing canonical contract at the plugin boundary (`CliBackendPlugin.parseJsonlEvent`), carries that function through backend resolution, and invokes it at the JSONL stdout parser source before downstream UI projection. It also routes plugin hook tool events through display-only runner callbacks so the documented contract matches runtime side effects. That fixes the ownership gap directly instead of adding a downstream text heuristic, a provider-specific special case for one CLI, or a trusted-delivery shortcut.
- **Fix classification:** Root cause fix
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for the plugin hook contract and parser/resolver behavior covered here. The only untested part is live manual rendering with a real external Cursor Agent CLI, which is intentionally outside this focused local regression scope.
- **Patch quality notes:** Patch-quality warnings about `return undefined`, `return false`, and `return true` are expected parser-control returns: `undefined` means a plugin hook did not recognize a line, `false` means no custom parser handled the line, and `true` means the custom event path consumed it. They are not fallback masking; they preserve existing built-in JSONL parsing for unrecognized lines. Daily-fix also reported a large diff/merge-risk view because `origin/main` moved after the task branch was created, but the actual task commit is focused on the CLI backend parser hook, related tests, SDK exports, and docs.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** The source-of-truth contract is the plugin SDK `CliBackendPlugin` type and the CLI JSONL parser boundary. The public contract surface is intentionally minimal and canonical: plugin code owns provider-specific wire parsing, OpenClaw owns normalized display events. Compatibility/default behavior is preserved because built-in `jsonlDialect` values and existing config schema remain unchanged. Related open PR / same-contract scan did not identify a conflicting canonical PR during this daily-fix flow. Out of scope: adding a Cursor-specific built-in dialect, widening user config schema, executing plugin-reported tools, or changing Claude/Gemini JSONL semantics.

target.title: fix(telegram): deliver reasoning as durable block when /reasoning on
target.body:
## Summary
Fix `/reasoning on` dropping the thinking lane on Telegram. When reasoning mode is "on" (not "stream"), the `onReasoningStream` callback was unconditionally `undefined` because both the reasoning draft stream and the progress draft path were inactive. This caused the model's thinking content to be silently lost — produced and archived, but never delivered to the Telegram chat.

The fix adds a third fallback branch that sends reasoning text as a durable (non-streaming) block message when `forceBlockStreamingForReasoning` is true. It also wires `onReasoningEnd` for the block-streaming path to reset progress state.

Fixes #94937

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Under `/reasoning on`, the model's `<think>` content is generated and archived but never rendered on Telegram. Under `/reasoning stream` the same content streams in an ephemeral block correctly.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node 24.13.1, Linux x64
- **Code analysis path**: See root cause analysis in issue comments (5 Whys)
- **Test framework**: Vitest

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/bot-message-dispatch.test.ts --run
```

**After-fix evidence**:
```
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  130 passed (130)
   Start at  22:19:21
   Duration  19.52s
```

**Observed result after the fix**: `onReasoningStream` callback is now defined when `/reasoning on` is active. The extracted and formatted reasoning text (e.g. `Thinking\n\n_ambient reasoning_`) is delivered via durable `sendPayload` instead of being silently dropped. No reasoning draft stream is created in "on" mode, matching intent.

**What was not tested**: Live Telegram end-to-end test with real model thinking output (requires configured bot + reasoning-capable model). The fix was verified at unit-test level and by tracing the full callback chain from `onReasoningStream` → `splitTelegramReasoningText` → `sendPayload` → `deliverInboundReplyWithMessageSendContext`.

## Tests and validation
| Test Type | Result | Details |
|-----------|--------|---------|
| Unit Tests | ✅ | 130 passed, includes new test for reasoning "on" durable delivery |
| Type Check | N/A | `tsgo` lanes only — not run locally per repo policy |
| Lint | N/A | `oxfmt`/`oxlint` config on file — no structural issues detected |

## Risk checklist
**Did user-visible behavior change?** `Yes` — `/reasoning on` now delivers thinking as persistent block messages on Telegram (previously silent drop).
**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** `No`
**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** `No`

## Current review state
**What is the next action?** Maintainer review requested

target.title: fix(telegram): delete approval message after resolution instead of clearing buttons
target.body:
## Summary

When a Telegram exec approval was resolved, `clearPendingActions` only cleared the inline keyboard — the approval prompt text remained visible in chat even after the user approved or denied.

This fix uses the approval runtime's built-in `kind: "delete"` final-action seam:

1. **`buildResolvedResult`**: Returns `{ kind: "delete" }` (was `{ kind: "clear-actions" }`), causing the shared runtime to call `transport.deleteEntry`
2. **`transport.deleteEntry`** (new): Calls `deleteMessageTelegram` to fully remove the approval bubble
3. **`clearPendingActions`** (restored): Only clears the inline keyboard for **expired** approvals — keeps the prompt text visible so chat history is preserved
4. **Type safety**: Added `deleteMessage` to `TelegramExecApprovalHandlerDeps`

Fixes #56286

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior addressed**: Telegram exec approval bubble was not dismissed after clicking approve/deny — only the buttons were cleared, leaving the prompt text.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node v24.13.0, Linux x86_64
- **Test framework**: Vitest v4.1.8 (5 approval handler tests)

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

```
 RUN  v4.1.8
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  5 passed (5)  ← 2 original + 3 new
   Start at  10:42:46
   Duration  17.54s
```

**New tests added** (3):

1. `returns delete for resolved and clear-actions for expired (regression #56286)` — Verifies `buildResolvedResult` → `{ kind: "delete" }` and `buildExpiredResult` → `{ kind: "clear-actions" }`

2. `deletes the approval message via transport.deleteEntry (regression #56286)` — Verifies `deleteEntry` calls `deleteMessageTelegram` with correct chatId, messageId, and options

3. `clears only the inline keyboard on expired, not delete (regression #56286)` — Verifies `clearPendingActions` calls `editMessageReplyMarkupTelegram` with empty buttons `[]` (preserves text) instead of deleting

**Observed result after the fix**: Resolved approvals are fully deleted from chat. Expired approvals only lose their buttons — the prompt text remains as chat history.

**What was not tested**: Live Telegram bot with real exec approval flow. The change is architecture-level (switching final-action kinds) and testable at the adapter layer via mocks.

## Tests and validation

```bash
# Targeted approval handler tests (5/5)
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  5 passed (5)
```

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes` — resolved approval bubbles now fully disappear instead of leaving text behind)

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`No`)

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`No`)

What is the highest-risk area?
- The `transport.deleteEntry` call relies on entry having `chatId`/`messageId` from `deliverPending`; if that contract changes, the delete fails gracefully

How is that risk mitigated?
- `deleteMessageTelegram` already handles delete-no-op errors gracefully (existing production code)
- `deleteEntry` falls back to `deleteMessageTelegram` if `deps?.deleteMessage` is absent
- `clearPendingActions` (expired path) is unchanged from original behavior — only clears buttons

## Changes from review feedback

Per ClawSweeper review on 2026-06-19:

1. ✅ **[P1] Keep clear-actions from deleting approval content**: `clearPendingActions` is now button-only (calls `editMessageReplyMarkupTelegram` with `[]`). Resolved approvals use `kind: "delete"` + `transport.deleteEntry` instead.

2. ✅ **[P1] Keep Telegram approval deps type-safe**: Added `deleteMessage` property to `TelegramExecApprovalHandlerDeps`. Restored `editMessageReplyMarkupTelegram` import. No type mismatches remain.

3. ✅ **[P1] Add focused cleanup tests**: Tests cover `buildResolvedResult`, `buildExpiredResult`, `transport.deleteEntry`, and `interactions.clearPendingActions` for resolved and expired paths.

## Current review state

What is the next action?
- Maintainer review

target.title: test(scripts): add check-package-dist-imports to golden test plan
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

The golden test `routes top-level test helpers to importing repo tests` in `src/scripts/test-projects.test.ts` fails on `main` because `test/scripts/check-package-dist-imports.test.ts` imports `test/helpers/temp-dir.ts` but is not listed in the expected golden output. This causes `checks-node-core-tooling` CI failures for all PRs.

## Why This Change Was Made

`test/scripts/check-package-dist-imports.test.ts` was added to the repository and imports `../helpers/temp-dir.js`. The `buildVitestRunPlans` function correctly includes it in the tooling config shard, but the golden test expectation was not updated to match.

## User Impact

Fixes `checks-node-core-tooling` CI failures across all open PRs.

- **After**: `pnpm test -- src/scripts/test-projects.test.ts` → 83 passed

target.title: Check ClawHub trust before plugin installs
target.body:
## Summary

Reapplies the ClawHub plugin-install trust check from #81307 after the revert in #81363.

This PR keeps the original trust-check scope:

- check ClawHub exact-release trust before ClawHub plugin downloads
- require interactive acknowledgement, or `--acknowledge-clawhub-risk`, for risky releases
- warn for pending/stale clean trust records without requiring acknowledgement
- fail closed when ClawHub security data is unavailable or malformed
- keep the existing local dangerous-code scanner intact

This does not close or replace the later scanner-removal discussion/PR.

## Rebase/current-main compatibility notes

This branch also carries narrow compatibility fixes needed after rebasing onto current `main`:

- preserve plugin runtime config scope for both current mutation helpers and deprecated compatibility helpers

target.title: feat(telegram): send voice typing cue before transcription

## Summary

- Send the initial Telegram direct-chat typing cue for audio-only voice messages before inbound body resolution starts audio transcription.
- Move configured binding readiness ahead of body/STT work so unavailable configured bindings do not emit preflight typing cues.
- Add and fix a typing-order regression test that proves `sendChatAction("typing")` runs before `transcribeFirstAudio` for voice-only direct messages.

Fixes #58887.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Telegram direct-chat voice messages now emit the first typing cue before audio transcription starts, so the sender gets immediate activity feedback during potentially slow STT work.

target.title: fix compaction timeout partial progress
target.body:
## Summary

- Preserve completed chunk summaries when a later compaction chunk times out, so timeout recovery can reuse partial progress instead of falling back to a generic “summary unavailable” note and restarting from chunk 1 on the next attempt.
- Preserve cooperative partial progress through the outer `compactWithSafetyTimeout()` wall-clock timeout path by allowing the canceled native compaction to settle with its cancellation-produced result before the timeout error wins the race.
- Wire the safety-timeout reason through the real native `activeSession.compact()` / `activeSession.abortCompaction(reason)` path, so `AgentSession` can commit a result that was produced by timeout cancellation while still treating non-timeout aborts as cancellation.
- Keep the timeout-result acceptance window alive through the native `AgentSession` post-await commit continuation, then expire it once the safety wrapper rejects so a late-settling timeout-aborted compaction cannot append session state after the run is already failed.
- Remove the old `src/node-host/with-timeout.ts` helper after this compaction path stopped using it, which fixes the `check-dependencies` deadcode CI failure for the updated branch.
- Keep caller abort cancellation semantics intact: non-timeout aborts still do not get converted into successful partial summaries.
- Surface compaction timeout details in the preflight failure reply, including the existing `agents.defaults.compaction.timeoutSeconds` knob, while generic non-verbose compaction failures still hide details.

Fixes #92043

## Maintainer-ready notes

- **Why it matters / User impact:** Users with large transcripts can hit the default compaction wall-clock timeout after some chunks already summarized successfully. Before this patch, that useful progress was discarded and the next attempt repeated the same earlier chunks, creating a permanent failure loop. The preflight reply also hid that the failure was a timeout, leaving users without the relevant config knob.
- **Fix classification:** Root-cause reliability fix for compaction timeout progress reuse and timeout failure copy.
- **Maintainer-ready confidence:** High for the local source/runtime surface covered here. The change has RED/GREEN regression coverage for inner chunk timeout, outer safety-timeout cancellation, and user-facing timeout copy; it preserves abort cancellation behavior and does not alter default timeout values or public config shape.
- **Root cause:** The root source was split across three timeout boundaries. `summarizeChunks()` discarded completed chunk summaries on timeout-shaped failures; the outer `compactWithSafetyTimeout()` could reject with its wall-clock timeout before a cooperating native compaction returned the partial result it produced during timeout cancellation; the real native `AgentSession` path aborted compaction without preserving the timeout reason, so a result produced after timeout cancellation was still classified as plain aborted work; the timeout-abort classification originally accepted any matching error name, which left a late-settling timeout result able to append after the safety wrapper had already rejected; and the first bounded-acceptance implementation expired the token before `AgentSession`'s post-await commit gate could observe it.
- **Why this is root-cause fix:** This changes the owners that enforce the partial-progress invariant: `src/agents/compaction.ts` carries partial summaries from inner chunk failures, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compaction-safety-timeout.ts` preserves the timeout reason, gives the native post-await commit continuation a bounded chance to return cancellation-produced progress before the timeout rejection wins, and expires timeout-result acceptance once the wrapper rejects; `src/agents/sessions/agent-session.ts` commits a compact result only while that timeout reason is still accepted instead of accepting arbitrary late timeout-named aborts. The copy change stays at the preflight compaction failure formatter.
- **What did NOT change:** Chunk planning, model retry policy, compaction timeout defaults, persistent session storage, provider routing, public config shape, and unrelated compaction behavior are unchanged and out of scope.
- **Architecture / source-of-truth check:** `src/agents/compaction.ts` remains the source-of-truth owner for summary partial-progress behavior, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compaction-safety-timeout.ts` owns the native compaction wall-clock race, and `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.ts` remains the source-of-truth owner for preflight agent-run failure copy.
- **Patch quality notes:** Timeout recovery is bounded to cooperative cancellation settlement at the timeout boundary and to the active safety-wrapper acceptance window through the native commit continuation. Non-timeout aborts still reject promptly, non-settling compactions still reject with `Compaction timed out`, late timeout-aborted results after wrapper rejection are ignored by `AgentSession`, and the default 180s timeout is unchanged.
- **Target test file:** `src/agents/compaction-partial-summary.test.ts`, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts`, `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts`, and `src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** Regression test scenarios: a two-chunk compaction where chunk 1 succeeds and chunk 2 throws `TimeoutError` must return the chunk 1 summary plus a `[Partial summary: ...]` marker; a compaction canceled by outer timeout and immediately resolving with progress must return that progress instead of `Compaction timed out`; direct native compaction passes the safety timeout reason into `activeSession.abortCompaction(reason)` so timeout-aborted results can be committed only while the safety wrapper still accepts that reason; a late result after wrapper rejection must not remain accepted; a preflight compaction timeout must surface the timeout reason and config guidance.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** The regression tests exercise the source owner boundaries directly and keep existing abort/timeout tests in place so timeout recovery cannot convert user cancellation or hung compaction into success.
- **Out of scope:** This PR does not change the default 180s timeout, add persistent checkpoint storage, change the shape/default of `agents.defaults.compaction.timeoutSeconds`, alter compaction chunk planning, or change model/provider retry policy.

## Root Cause

`src/agents/compaction.ts` treated timeout errors the same as cancellation and rethrew immediately before attaching the already-generated summary to the error. `summarizeWithFallback()` therefore had no partial summary to reuse, even when earlier chunks had already completed successfully.

The outer safety timeout added a second loss path: `compactWithSafetyTimeout()` raced native compaction against a timeout rejection. On timeout it aborted the active compaction, but the timeout rejection could win before the native compaction settled with the partial result it produced during that cancellation.

The native caller added a third loss path: `compactEmbeddedAgentSessionDirectOnce()` canceled the real `AgentSession` via `activeSession.abortCompaction()` with no timeout reason, and `AgentSession.runCompactionWork()` discarded any compact result once its internal compaction signal was aborted. Timeout cancellation and user/request abort therefore collapsed into the same aborted state.

Separately, `buildPreflightCompactionFailureText()` hid the preflight compaction reason unless verbose details were enabled. That made a 180s compaction timeout look like a generic unrecoverable compaction failure and did not point users at the configured timeout knob.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Later chunk timeout and outer wall-clock safety timeout no longer discard already-generated compaction summary progress; preflight compaction timeout replies now show the timeout reason and `agents.defaults.compaction.timeoutSeconds` guidance.
- **Real environment tested:** Local Linux OpenClaw worktrees. Original wall-clock/runtime proof was collected on `/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043`, branch `feat/issue-92043`; latest conflict-resolution verification was collected on `/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-92899`, rebased head `cdacf1e8e868aa6e763f68cc2a2611153a06437f` against `origin/main` `f9fc2efe681aa1975319c854d9dc6216e6b08935`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043 && OPENCLAW_REPO=/media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043 node --import tsx /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-92043-evidence/compaction-real-wallclock-proof.mjs > /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-issue-92043-evidence/compaction-real-wallclock-proof.out.json
```

```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043 && OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=240000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts -t "keeps timeout acceptance alive through the native commit continuation"
```

```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043 && OPENCLAW_TEST_FAST=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_NO_OUTPUT_TIMEOUT_MS=240000 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts src/agents/compaction-partial-summary.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts
```

```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043 && node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --noEmit
```

```bash
cd /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043 && ./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compaction-safety-timeout.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts
```

```bash
git -C /media/vdc/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/issue-92043 diff --check
```

```bash
git -C "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-92899" diff --check
```

```bash
cd "/media/vdb/code/ai/aispace/openclaw-worktrees/pr-92899" && node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts src/agents/compaction-partial-summary.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts
```

- **Evidence after fix:**

```json
{
  "proof": "compaction-real-wallclock-timeout",
  "head": "5013dda6bd70253d7614c12257a8e7a3b0e94c03",
  "timerMode": "real setTimeout; no fake timers; no Vitest",
  "timeoutMs": 120,
  "elapsedMs": 123,
  "result": "partial summary after chunk 1 survived real wall-clock safety timeout",
  "events": [
    { "event": "compaction-start", "aborted": false },
    {
      "event": "timeout-abort-observed",
      "reasonName": "CompactionTimeoutError",
      "reasonMessage": "Compaction timed out"
    },
    {
      "event": "onCancel",
      "reasonName": "CompactionTimeoutError",
      "reasonMessage": "Compaction timed out"
    },
    { "event": "partial-summary-committed-after-timeout-abort" }
  ]
}
```

```text
Test Files  2 passed (2)
Tests  2 passed | 48 skipped (50)
```

```text
Test Files  3 passed (3)
Tests  103 passed (103)
[test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 20.81s
```

```text
node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json --noEmit exited 0 with no stdout/stderr output.
```

```text
All matched files use the correct format.
Finished in 21ms on 2 files using 1 threads.
```

```text
git diff --check exited 0 with no whitespace errors.
```

```text
2026-06-18 rebase verification on head cdacf1e8e868aa6e763f68cc2a2611153a06437f:
- git diff --check: passed with no output
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts src/agents/compaction-partial-summary.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-direct-runtime-config.test.ts
- [test] passed 2 Vitest shards in 197.93s
- Test Files: 4 passed
- Tests: 109 passed
```

- **Observed result after fix:** A later timeout after chunk 1 completes returns the chunk 1 summary with a `[Partial summary: chunks 1-1 of 2 were summarized...]` marker instead of the generic unavailable summary. The real wall-clock proof ran the production `compactWithSafetyTimeout()` wrapper outside Vitest/fake timers, observed a real timeout abort with `CompactionTimeoutError`, called `onCancel` with that reason, and returned `partial summary after chunk 1 survived real wall-clock safety timeout`. The direct native caller regression verifies the safety timeout reason is passed into `activeSession.abortCompaction(reason)`, and the new native commit-continuation regression verifies that a result produced after timeout cancellation still sees its timeout reason accepted at the `AgentSession`-shaped post-await gate before the safety wrapper rejects. The focused late-result regression remains covered by the full compaction suite, verifying acceptance expires after wrapper rejection so a late timeout-aborted result cannot append session state after the timed-out run has already failed. Hung compactions still reject with `Compaction timed out`; run-level aborts still reject as aborts. After rebasing onto `origin/main` `f9fc2efe681aa1975319c854d9dc6216e6b08935`, the conflict-resolved exact head `cdacf1e8e868aa6e763f68cc2a2611153a06437f` still passes the focused compaction safety, partial summary, native compact hook, and direct runtime config regression files (109 tests).
- **What was not tested:** No live external model provider, full 180s gateway compaction, Desktop session, or external channel transport was exercised in this latest rebase pass. The prior proof uses the production safety-timeout wrapper with real wall-clock timers and no Vitest/fake timers; the native `activeSession.abortCompaction(reason)` handoff and current conflict-resolved head remain covered by focused direct-runtime regression tests.

## Review findings addressed

- **RF-001 [P2]: bounded timeout-aborted native compaction commit window.** This remains an intentional, narrow runtime policy for maintainer acceptance. The accepted reason is a `WeakSet` entry created by the safety wrapper's own timeout error, not any error with a matching name. Current-head regressions cover both the allowed native post-await commit continuation (`keeps timeout acceptance alive through the native commit continuation`) and expiry after the safety wrapper rejects (`expires timeout-result acceptance after the safety wrapper rejects`).
- **RF-002 [P1]: no live slow-provider/full 180-second gateway compaction.** Still disclosed. The latest rebase pass did not run a live provider or full 180s gateway scenario; current confidence comes from the prior real wall-clock safety-wrapper proof, source tracing, exact-head focused regressions, and CI-visible branch checks.

## Regression Test Plan

- `src/agents/compaction-partial-summary.test.ts` locks the inner timeout case: once a chunk has completed, a later timeout preserves the latest summary and partial marker.
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner.compaction-safety-timeout.test.ts` locks the outer safety-timeout race: timeout cancellation can return cooperative partial progress before the timeout error wins, native post-await commit continuation still sees an accepted timeout reason, timeout/external abort reasons are threaded into the cancel hook, and timeout-result acceptance expires once the wrapper rejects.
- `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts` locks the real direct native caller: safety timeout cancellation passes the timeout reason into `activeSession.abortCompaction(reason)` before the native compact result is returned.
- The existing abort and hung-timeout tests remain green: non-timeout aborts still do not get converted into partial-success summaries, and non-settling compactions still reject with timeout.

## Merge risk

- **Risk labels considered:** session-state, reliability, user-facing copy, timeout handling.
- **Risk explanation:** The behavior change is limited to compaction timeout errors after useful progress exists or when a cooperating compaction settles because of timeout cancellation before the safety wrapper rejects. First-chunk failures still fall through to the existing generic fallback, hung compactions still time out, late timeout-aborted results after wrapper rejection are ignored, and non-timeout aborts still propagate as cancellation.
- **Why acceptable:** Reusing completed chunk summaries makes repeated compaction attempts move forward instead of discarding progress. The copy change only reveals timeout reasons for compaction timeout failures; generic non-verbose compaction errors remain redacted.
- **Out-of-scope boundary:** This PR does not change the default 180s timeout, add persistent checkpoint storage, alter compaction chunk planning, or change model/provider retry policy.

target.title: fix(memory): repair Windows QMD paths whose backslashes were stripped by JSON parsing
target.body:
## Summary

On Windows, QMD memory backend was completely unusable regardless of how `memory.qmd.command` was configured. The configured path had its backslashes stripped by JSON parsing (since `\` is an escape character in JSON), producing a drive-relative path like `C:Users...`. Node.js `path.isAbsolute()` returns `false` for drive-relative paths, so `resolvePath` treated the mangled path as relative and joined it with the workspace directory — yielding `C:\Users\<user>\.openclaw\workspace\Users<user>AppDataRoamingnpmnode_modules...`.

This fix adds drive-relative path detection to `resolvePath` in the memory backend config. When a path matches the pattern `^[A-Za-z]:[^\\/]` (a Windows drive letter + colon with no directory separator following), it repairs the path by inserting a `\` separator after the drive letter, so it resolves as absolute instead of relative.

Additionally, the `command` field is now resolved through `resolvePath` so it benefits from the same repair.

Fixes #92302

## What Problem This Solves

Windows users cannot use the QMD memory backend because configured paths are silently corrupted by JSON parsing. The index stays at 0/N files, and every `memory index --force` or `memory search` fails with `Cannot find module` referencing a workspace-concatenated path. The only workaround is to switch to `memory.backend = "builtin"`.

## Evidence

The fix adds a regex-based detection in `resolvePath` (2 lines at the point where `path.isAbsolute` returned false) and routes the repaired path through the absolute-path normalization path instead of the relative-path join.

Existing tests (26) pass unchanged. A new test covers the repair for both `command` and collection `paths` entries.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed**: Windows paths in `memory.qmd.command` and `memory.qmd.paths[].path` have their backslashes stripped by JSON parsing, producing drive-relative paths that `path.isAbsolute()` rejects. These fall through to `path.resolve(workspaceDir, ...)`, joining them with the workspace directory.

**Real setup tested**:
- **Runtime**: Node.js v24.13.1, Linux x86_64 (behavior is platform-dependent; the regex repair is universal)
- **Test framework**: Vitest v4.1.8
- **Branch**: `fix/issue-92302-qmd-windows-path`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
cd /home/0668001085/workspace/openclaw
git checkout fix/issue-92302-qmd-windows-path
npx vitest run packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/backend-config.test.ts
```

**After-fix evidence**:

The key code change in `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/backend-config.ts`:

```typescript
function resolvePath(raw: string, workspaceDir: string): string {
  const trimmed = raw.trim();
  if (!trimmed) { throw new Error("path required"); }
  if (trimmed.startsWith("~") || path.isAbsolute(trimmed)) {
    return path.normalize(resolveUserPath(trimmed));
  }
  // FIX #92302: Detect Windows drive-relative paths (C:Users) whose
  // backslash after the colon was stripped by JSON parsing.
  const driveRelative = /^[A-Za-z]:([^\\/])/.exec(trimmed);
  if (driveRelative) {
    const repaired = `${trimmed.slice(0, 2)}\\${trimmed.slice(2)}`;
    return path.normalize(resolveUserPath(repaired));
  }
  return path.normalize(path.resolve(workspaceDir, trimmed));
}
```

Before/after for a post-JSON-parsing corrupted path:

| Input (after JSON) | Before fix | After fix |
|---|---|---|
| `C:Toolsqmd.js` | `C:\workspace\root\C:Toolsqmd.js` ❌ | `C:\Toolsqmd.js` ✅ |
| `C:MyDriveVault` | `C:\workspace\root\C:MyDriveVault` ❌ | `C:\MyDriveVault` ✅ |
| `notes` (relative) | `C:\workspace\root\notes` ✅ | unchanged ✅ |

**Observed result after the fix**: Drive-relative paths that match the pattern of a JSON-corrupted Windows absolute path are automatically repaired and resolved as absolute paths. Legitimate relative paths (without a drive letter prefix) continue to be joined with the workspace directory.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end QMD indexing on a Windows machine with the actual `@tobilu/qmd` package. The fix is at the config resolution layer; it corrects a deterministic path-mangling bug that is reproducible from the error messages reported in the issue.

## Risk checklist

**Did user-visible behavior change?** `No` — only changes how QMD paths are resolved. The QMD backend was completely non-functional on Windows before this fix.

**Did config, environment, or migration behavior change?** `No`.

**Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change?** `No`.

**What is the highest-risk area?** A legitimate relative path that starts with a drive letter pattern (e.g., a directory literally named `C:foo` inside the workspace) would be incorrectly treated as absolute.

**How is that risk mitigated?** Directory names with colons are invalid on Windows, so a legitimate relative path cannot start with `X:`. On Unix, the repaired path would still be invalid (causing a clear error), not silently resolved to a wrong location.

## Current review state

**What is the next action?** Maintainer review requested.

target.title: docs: add Plugin Inspector development and CI workflow
target.body:
## Summary

- Add a canonical Plugin Inspector guide for plugin-author development, CI, trusted runtime capture, beta-checkout comparison, reports, and plugin-shipped skill proof.
- Reorder the plugin testing guide so external authors see package, runtime, and live-test guidance before OpenClaw's repo-only test helper reference.
- Link the workflow from the plugin quickstart and tool-plugin CI guide, and replace the stale future-tense Inspector section in the compatibility reference.
- Keep the full Plugin Inspector command/API reference in its owner repository; this PR documents how it fits into OpenClaw plugin development.

## Linked context

Related: https://github.com/openclaw/clawhub/pull/2627

Requested during the ClawHub/OpenClaw plugin documentation consolidation work.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: OpenClaw's canonical docs did not explain how external plugin authors should use Plugin Inspector during development and CI, while the testing page led with repo-only helpers and the compatibility page described the Inspector as future work.
- Real environment tested: OpenClaw docs checkout at `2ab6caeb80` and the published `@openclaw/plugin-inspector@0.3.15` CLI.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: `npx --yes @openclaw/plugin-inspector@0.3.15 --help`; `pnpm docs:list`; `pnpm format:docs:check`; `pnpm docs:check-mdx`; `pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary` with the new page included; `pnpm lint:docs`; `pnpm docs:check-links`; `git diff --check`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): `pnpm docs:list` lists `plugins/plugin-inspector.md`; the current Inspector CLI help confirms the documented `check`, `inspect`, `ci`, `config`, `init`, checkout, runtime, author-facing, SARIF, and JUnit surfaces; all listed repository docs checks pass.
- Observed result after fix: the new page is discoverable in navigation and passes OpenClaw's formatting, MDX, glossary, lint, JSON, and internal-link checks.
- What was not tested: a completed local Mintlify render and Mintlify anchor audit.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: the local Mintlify preview and `mint broken-links --check-anchors` stop on existing parse errors in unrelated current-main pages under `docs/plugins/reference/`; the pnpm anchor runner also hits an isolated Mintlify `react` peer-resolution error.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): `docs/plugins/compatibility.md` used a future-tense day-one CLI and stale `@openclaw/plugin-inspector@0.1.0` examples; `docs/plugins/sdk-testing.md` started with repo-only bundled-plugin helpers.

## Tests and validation

- `pnpm format:docs:check`
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx`
- `pnpm docs:check-i18n-glossary` with the new page included through a temporary index
- `pnpm lint:docs`
- `pnpm docs:check-links`
- `pnpm docs:list`
- `git diff --check`
- `git merge-tree --write-tree upstream/main HEAD`

No runtime test was added because this is a docs-only change.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`, documentation only.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No`.

What is the highest-risk area? Inaccurate or unsafe Plugin Inspector command guidance.

How is that risk mitigated? The workflow is grounded in the current published Inspector README, package metadata, CLI help, and CLI source. Runtime capture is explicitly separated and limited to trusted code.

## Current review state

Ready for maintainer review and CI. No known author-side changes remain.

target.title: fix(memory): search memory and wiki concurrently for corpus=all (#92633)
target.body:
### Summary

- `memory_search` with `corpus=all` ran the builtin memory/sessions branch and the wiki/supplement branch **sequentially** under one 15s tool deadline. Two searches that each finished well under 15s individually summed past the deadline, so `corpus=all` consistently returned `results: []` with `disabled: true` while `corpus=memory`, `corpus=sessions`, `corpus=wiki`, and the CLI all succeeded.
- This change starts the wiki/supplement search concurrently with the memory/sessions search inside the same deadline. The combined wall time drops from `memory + wiki` to roughly `max(memory, wiki)`, so `corpus=all` fits the deadline and returns merged results.
- The two branches share no mutable state, so overlapping them is safe; result merging, corpus balancing, the cooldown decision, and paused-index handling are unchanged.

### Root cause

In the memory_search tool runner, the supplement branch was awaited only after the memory branch fully resolved (manager fetch → vector search → optional forced resync → visibility filtering → decoration). The wiki/supplement branch then performed its own independent embedding and search. Under a single shared 15s deadline the two latencies added together, so an aggregation that was healthy per-corpus timed out as a whole and was misreported as an embedding/provider failure.

### Fix

- Kick off the supplement-search promise before the memory branch begins and await it at the existing merge point, so both branches run within the same deadline window concurrently.
- Keep the supplement branch out of the memory cooldown phase tracker so the `corpus=all` cooldown attribution stays single-writer and deterministic: a wiki failure can no longer race a real memory-backend failure into skipping the cooldown.
- Guard the hoisted promise so an early paused-index return cannot leave it rejected-but-unobserved.
- No change to result merging, corpus balancing, cooldown recording semantics, or the unavailable/paused result shapes.

### Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed: `memory_search({ corpus: "all" })` no longer times out and returns empty when the memory and wiki branches each complete within the deadline but sum past it; it now returns merged memory + wiki results, and a real memory-backend failure still records the cooldown even when the wiki branch fails too.**

**Real environment tested (memory-core extension Vitest suite, Linux, Node 24): a deterministic concurrency regression test drives the real tool runner with a memory search and a registered wiki supplement that each take 9s under fake timers, plus a both-branches-throw test for the cooldown attribution.**

**Exact steps or command run after this patch (`pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts extensions/memory-core/src/tools.test.ts`): the new cases `runs the memory and wiki branches concurrently for corpus=all (#92633)` and `records the memory cooldown when corpus=all memory and wiki both fail (#92633)`, plus the full files.**

**Evidence after fix (advancing fake time by 9s resolves the tool with both corpora): `corpus=all` returns `[["wiki","entities/alpha.md"],["memory","MEMORY.md"]]` with `disabled` undefined; the both-fail case still records the memory cooldown so the next memory search is suppressed. All 39 cases across the two suites pass.**

**Observed result after fix (the same two 9s branches that summed to 18s sequentially now complete at ~9s): with the production change reverted, the identical concurrency test hangs until the 120s test timeout because the wiki branch only starts after the memory branch finishes; with the change it resolves at 9s.**

**What was not tested (a live gateway against a real OpenAI-compatible embedding endpoint, `text-embedding-v4`, and a real compiled-wiki supplement on Windows Server): the reporter's exact environment was not reproduced; proof is the deterministic two-slow-branch simulation of the shared-deadline path.**

### Reproduction

1. Configure `memory_search` with a healthy embedding provider and at least one registered wiki/compiled-wiki supplement, where the builtin memory/sessions search and the wiki search each take several seconds.
2. Call `memory_search({ query, corpus: "memory" })`, `corpus: "sessions"`, and `corpus: "wiki"` — each succeeds within the 15s deadline.
3. Call `memory_search({ query, corpus: "all" })`.
4. **Before this PR**: the memory and wiki branches run one after the other, their latencies add up past 15s, and the tool returns `results: []`, `disabled: true`, `error: "memory_search timed out after 15s"`.
5. **After this PR**: both branches run concurrently within the deadline, the tool returns the merged memory + wiki results.

### Risk / Mitigation

- Risk: concurrent execution could race shared per-call state. Mitigation: the branches write disjoint locals (memory result/provider/debug vs. supplement results), and the supplement branch is deliberately kept out of the cooldown phase tracker so that tracker has a single writer.
- Risk: a hoisted supplement promise could become an unobserved rejection if the memory branch returns early (paused index). Mitigation: the promise is guarded with a no-op `catch`, and the real rejection still surfaces at the awaited merge point on the normal path.
- Risk: a genuinely hung wiki supplement could still trip the deadline. Mitigation: unchanged from today — a non-resolving branch still times out, and the existing stall tests continue to pass.

### Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue)
- [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality)
- [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected)
- [ ] Documentation update
- [ ] Refactor / internal change

### Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Memory / dreaming (`extensions/memory-core`)
- [ ] Core runtime / gateway
- [ ] Channels / plugins
- [ ] Config / migrations
- [ ] Docs

### Linked Issue/PR

Fixes #92633
